title: Heuristics for Goal Progress
concept: Detecting when an agent is stuck or making zero progress across turns, and triggering self-correction prompts.

exercises.prompt: Last lesson: a heuristic safety check runs BEFORE an action executes. What is its main job?

options.text: Catch broken formats or illegal arguments before they run
Make the agent respond faster to the user
Give the agent a better personality

exercises.explain: A safety check is a quick pre-filter. It inspects a proposed action and blocks obvious problems (bad format, illegal values) before the action ever executes.

cards.title: Going in Circles
cards.body: Imagine an agent trying the same failed step over and over, never getting closer to the goal. It is stuck! A smart agent should notice this and change plans.

cards.title: Progress Heuristic
cards.body: A progress heuristic is a simple rule that checks if the agent is actually getting closer to its goal each turn. If nothing changes for several turns, it flags 'no progress'.

cards.title: Lost on a Hike
cards.body: If you pass the same funny-shaped rock three times, you know you are walking in circles. That repeated landmark is your clue to stop and re-check the map.

cards.title: Stuck Agent Fix
cards.body: An agent runs the same search query 4 turns in a row with no new results. A heuristic spots the repeat and triggers a self-correction prompt: 'This isn't working, try a different approach.'

cards.title: Myth: It Just Knows
cards.body: Agents don't magically sense they're stuck. Without a progress check, they can loop forever. You must give them explicit rules to detect zero progress.

cards.title: Progress vs. Stuck
cards.body: Compare an agent making progress to one spinning its wheels.

left.text: Each turn changes state and moves closer to the goal.

right.text: Turns repeat with no new info; trigger self-correction.

exercises.prompt: An agent has made the same tool call with identical arguments for 5 turns and gotten identical results. What should a progress heuristic do?

options.text: Flag no progress and trigger a self-correction prompt
Keep repeating the call until it works

exercises.explain: Identical calls with identical results across many turns is the classic 'stuck' signal. The heuristic should stop the loop and prompt the agent to try a new approach.

exercises.prompt: True or False: A progress heuristic mainly checks whether the agent's state is actually changing toward the goal across turns.
exercises.explain: Correct! Progress heuristics watch for change over time. If nothing meaningfully changes, that's the warning sign the agent is stuck.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank about what to do when an agent is stuck.
exercises.sentence: When no progress is detected across turns, the agent should trigger a self-correction prompt to try a new approach.
exercises.explain: Detecting zero progress is only useful if it leads to action. A self-correction prompt tells the agent to change strategy instead of looping.

exercises.prompt: Which self-correction prompt sounds AI-generated for a stuck agent?

options.text: Your last 3 attempts repeated with no new results. Reflect on why they failed and propose a different strategy before continuing.

exercises.explain: The AI-style prompt is clear, structured, and actionable: it names the problem (repeated attempts) and directs a specific next step (reflect, then change strategy).

exercises.prompt: An agent must book a flight but its booking tool keeps returning the same 'invalid date' error for 4 turns while it retries unchanged. Which combination best handles this?

options.text: A progress heuristic detects the repeated failure and triggers a self-correction prompt to fix the date format and try a new approach
Ignore the errors and retry the exact same call forever
Immediately delete the goal since it failed once
Make the same call faster to save time

exercises.explain: Good agents pair detection with action: a progress heuristic notices the repeated, unchanging failure (zero progress), then fires a self-correction prompt so the agent reflects and changes its strategy instead of looping. Detecting stuck states and self-correcting is what keeps agents from wasting turns forever.
